profile: My name is Jane Eyre, an introspective and principled young woman who values integrity, emotional strength, and independence. I stay true to myself and make thoughtful choices based on what I believe is right.

events.semantic_description: (Day 0, t=0) I was glad that I could not take a walk that day due to the cold winter wind and rain.
(Day 0, t=1) I thought about how I never liked long walks, especially on chilly afternoons, and how dreadful it was to come home in the raw twilight.
(Day 0, t=2) I felt saddened by the chidings of Bessie, the nurse, and humbled by my physical inferiority to Eliza, John, and Georgiana Reed.
(Day 0, t=3) I observed Eliza, John, and Georgiana clustered around their mama in the drawing-room, where she lay reclined on a sofa by the fireside.
(Day 0, t=4) I heard Mrs. Reed say that she regretted keeping me at a distance until she could see that I was trying to acquire a more sociable and childlike disposition.
(Day 0, t=5) I asked Mrs. Reed what Bessie said I had done.
(Day 0, t=6) I was told by Mrs. Reed to be seated somewhere and remain silent until I could speak pleasantly.
(Day 0, t=7) I slipped into the breakfast-room, which adjoined the drawing-room.
(Day 0, t=8) I possessed myself of a volume from the bookcase, ensuring it was one stored with pictures.
(Day 0, t=9) I mounted into the window-seat, gathered up my feet, and sat cross-legged like a Turk.
(Day 0, t=10) I drew the red moreen curtain nearly close, creating a sense of double retirement.
(Day 0, t=11) I studied the aspect of the drear November day while turning over the leaves of my book.
(Day 0, t=12) I observed a pale blank of mist and cloud afar, and a scene of wet lawn and storm-beat shrub nearby.
(Day 0, t=13) I returned to my book, Bewick’s History of British Birds, and noted that I cared little for the letterpress.
(Day 0, t=14) I could not pass over certain introductory pages that treated of the haunts of sea-fowl.
(Day 0, t=15) I formed an idea of my own about the death-white realms described in the book, which were shadowy yet strangely impressive.
(Day 0, t=16) I felt haunted by the solitary churchyard with its inscribed headstone and low horizon.
(Day 0, t=17) I believed the two ships becalmed on a torpid sea to be marine phantoms.
(Day 0, t=18) I passed over the fiend pinning down the thief’s pack quickly, as it was an object of terror.
(Day 0, t=19) I found each picture in the book to tell a story that was mysterious yet profoundly interesting.
(Day 0, t=20) I recalled how interesting Bessie's tales were when she was in good humour, as she narrated passages of love and adventure.
(Day 0, t=21) I felt happy with Bewick on my knee, fearing only interruption.
(Day 0, t=22) I heard the breakfast-room door open and recognized John Reed's voice calling out.
(Day 0, t=23) I thought it was well that I drew the curtain, wishing fervently that John would not discover my hiding place.
(Day 0, t=24) I came out immediately when Eliza announced my location to John.
(Day 0, t=25) I asked John what he wanted with awkward diffidence.
(Day 0, t=26) I approached John's chair when he gestured for me to stand before him.
(Day 0, t=27) I mused on John's disgusting and ugly appearance while dreading the blow I knew would come.
(Day 0, t=28) I felt a sudden and strong strike from John, causing me to totter and step back.
(Day 0, t=29) I said to John that he was wicked and cruel, comparing him to a murderer and a slave-driver.
(Day 0, t=30) I expressed my thoughts about John being like the Roman emperors after reading Goldsmith’s History of Rome.
(Day 0, t=31) I felt blood trickle down my neck from a cut on my head after John struck me with a book.
(Day 0, t=32) I received John's attack in frantic sort, feeling a mix of pain and anger.
(Day 0, t=33) I heard Eliza and Georgiana run for Mrs. Reed as John called me names.
(Day 0, t=34) I was parted from John when Mrs. Reed arrived with Bessie and her maid Abbot.
(Day 0, t=35) I heard Mrs. Reed say to take me away to the red-room and lock me in there.
(Day 0, t=36) I was borne upstairs by four hands.
(Day 1, t=0) I resisted all the way, feeling a new thing for me, as Bessie and Miss Abbot were disposed to entertain a bad opinion of me.
(Day 1, t=1) I was conscious that a moment’s mutiny had rendered me liable to strange penalties, feeling resolved to go all lengths in my desperation.
(Day 1, t=2) I was held by Bessie and Miss Abbot, who described me as 'like a mad cat.'
(Day 1, t=3) I heard Bessie cry, 'What shocking conduct, Miss Eyre, to strike a young gentleman, your benefactress’s son!'
(Day 1, t=4) I questioned, 'Master! How is he my master? Am I a servant?'
(Day 1, t=5) I was told by Miss Abbot that I was 'less than a servant' and that I should sit down and think over my wickedness.
(Day 1, t=6) I was thrust upon a stool in the apartment indicated by Mrs. Reed, and I felt an impulse to rise from it.
(Day 1, t=7) I was arrested instantly by their hands when I tried to rise.
(Day 1, t=8) I heard Bessie say, 'If you don’t sit still, you must be tied down.'
(Day 1, t=9) I cried, 'Don’t take them off; I will not stir,' as I attached myself to my seat by my hands.
(Day 1, t=10) I noticed Bessie loosen her hold of me after ascertaining that I was subsiding.
(Day 1, t=11) I observed Bessie and Miss Abbot standing with folded arms, looking darkly and doubtfully on my face.
(Day 1, t=12) I heard Bessie say, 'She never did so before,' turning to Miss Abbot.
(Day 1, t=13) I listened as Miss Abbot replied, 'But it was always in her.'
(Day 1, t=14) I heard Bessie tell me that I ought to be aware I was under obligations to Mrs. Reed, who keeps me.
(Day 1, t=15) I felt a vague sense of pain as Bessie said that if Mrs. Reed turned me off, I would have to go to the poorhouse. For the first time I felt myself to be truly poor.
(Day 1, t=16) I had nothing to say to Bessie's words, as they were not new to me.
(Day 1, t=17) I listened as Miss Abbot told me I ought not to think myself on an equality with the Misses Reed and Master Reed.
(Day 1, t=18) I heard Bessie say, 'What we tell you is for your good,' and I felt her tone was not harsh.
(Day 1, t=19) I listened as Miss Abbot warned me that God would punish me if I did not repent.
(Day 1, t=20) I felt a sense of dread as Miss Abbot said something bad might come down the chimney and fetch me away.
(Day 1, t=21) I was left alone in the red-room after Miss Abbot shut and locked the door. A feeling of fear and oppression toward Mrs. Reed came over me.
(Day 1, t=22) I observed the red-room, noting it was seldom slept in and one of the largest and stateliest chambers in the mansion.
(Day 1, t=23) I saw a bed supported on massive pillars of mahogany, hung with curtains of deep red damask.
(Day 1, t=24) I noticed the two large windows with their blinds always drawn down, half shrouded in drapery.
(Day 1, t=25) I saw the carpet was red and the table at the foot of the bed was covered with a crimson cloth.
(Day 1, t=26) I observed the walls were a soft fawn color with a blush of pink in it.
(Day 1, t=27) I noted the wardrobe, toilet-table, and chairs were of darkly polished old mahogany.
(Day 1, t=28) I saw the piled-up mattresses and pillows of the bed, spread with a snowy Marseilles counterpane.
(Day 1, t=29) I noticed an ample cushioned easy-chair near the head of the bed, which looked like a pale throne.
(Day 1, t=30) I felt the room was chill because it seldom had a fire.
(Day 1, t=31) I perceived the room was silent, remote from the nursery and kitchen.
(Day 1, t=32) I felt the room was solemn, known to be seldom entered.
(Day 1, t=33) I recalled that the house-maid came here on Saturdays to wipe dust from the mirrors and furniture.
(Day 1, t=34) I remembered Mrs. Reed visited it at far intervals to review the contents of a secret drawer.
(Day 1, t=35) I sat on a low ottoman near the marble chimney-piece, looking at the bed before me.
(Day 1, t=36) I looked to my right hand at the high, dark wardrobe and to my left at the muffled windows.
(Day 1, t=37) I crossed before the looking-glass and explored the depth it revealed.
(Day 1, t=38) I saw my fascinated glance at the strange little figure gazing back at me, with a white face and arms specking the gloom.
(Day 1, t=39) I returned to my stool after observing my reflection.
(Day 1, t=40) I felt a rush of retrospective thought before I quailed to the dismal present.
(Day 1, t=41) I reflected on all John Reed’s violent tyrannies and my suffering.
(Day 1, t=42) I questioned why I was always browbeaten and accused, feeling it was useless to try to win anyone’s favor.
(Day 1, t=43) I thought about how Eliza and Georgiana were respected and indulged despite their faults.
(Day 1, t=44) I felt my head still ached and bled from the blow and fall I had received.
(Day 1, t=45) I felt a sense of injustice as I thought no one reproved John for striking me.
(Day 1, t=46) I thought of some strange expedient to escape from insupportable oppression, like running away or starving myself.
(Day 1, t=47) I felt a consternation of soul that dreary afternoon, with my brain in tumult and heart in insurrection.
(Day 1, t=48) I reflected on my discord in Gateshead Hall, feeling I was like nobody there.
(Day 1, t=49) I felt my habitual mood of humiliation and self-doubt fall damp on my decaying ire.
(Day 1, t=50) I heard the rain beating continuously on the staircase window and the wind howling in the grove.
(Day 1, t=51) I grew cold as a stone and felt my courage sink.
(Day 1, t=52) I questioned whether I was fit to die or if the vault under the chancel of Gateshead Church was inviting.
(Day 1, t=53) I recalled that Mr. Reed had taken me in as a parentless infant and required a promise from Mrs. Reed.
(Day 1, t=54) I thought about how irksome it must have been for Mrs. Reed to keep her promise to a strange child.
(Day 1, t=55) I had a singular notion that if Mr. Reed had been alive, he would have treated me kindly.
(Day 1, t=56) I sat looking at the white bed and overshadowed walls, recalling ideas of dead men troubling their graves.
(Day 1, t=57) I wiped my tears and hushed my sobs, fearful of showing violent grief.
(Day 1, t=58) I tried to look boldly round the dark room, shaking my hair from my eyes.
(Day 1, t=59) I saw a light gleam on the wall and questioned if it was a ray from the moon.
(Day 1, t=60) I gazed at the light as it glided up to the ceiling and quivered over my head.
(Day 1, t=61) I felt my heart beat thick and my head grow hot as I thought the light was a herald of a coming vision.
(Day 1, t=62) I felt oppressed and suffocated, and my endurance broke down.
(Day 1, t=63) I rushed to the door and shook the lock in a desperate effort.
(Day 1, t=64) I heard steps running along the outer passage before Bessie and Abbot entered.
(Day 1, t=65) I cried, 'Take me out! Let me go into the nursery!'
(Day 1, t=66) I explained to Bessie that I saw a light and thought a ghost would come.
(Day 1, t=67) I held Bessie’s hand and felt she did not snatch it from me.
(Day 1, t=68) I heard Abbot declare that I screamed out on purpose, expressing disgust.
(Day 1, t=69) I heard Mrs. Reed demand to know what was happening as she came along the corridor.
(Day 1, t=70) I pleaded with Mrs. Reed, 'O aunt! have pity! Forgive me! I cannot endure it—let me be punished some other way!'
(Day 1, t=71) I felt frantic anguish and wild sobs as Mrs. Reed thrust me back and locked me in.
(Day 1, t=72) I heard Mrs. Reed sweeping away after locking me in, and I suppose I had a species of fit.
(Day 1, t=73) I felt unconsciousness close the scene.
(Day 2, t=0) I woke up feeling as if I had had a frightful nightmare and saw a terrible red glare crossed with thick black bars.
(Day 2, t=1) I heard voices speaking with a hollow sound, as if muffled by a rush of wind or water, and felt agitation, uncertainty, and terror.
(Day 2, t=2) I became aware that someone was handling me, lifting me up and supporting me in a sitting posture more tenderly than I had ever been raised before.
(Day 2, t=3) I rested my head against a pillow or an arm and felt easy.
(Day 2, t=4) I realized I was in my own bed and that the red glare was the nursery fire; it was night.
(Day 2, t=5) I saw a candle burning on the table, Bessie standing at the bed-foot with a basin in her hand, and a gentleman sitting in a chair near my pillow, leaning over me.
(Day 2, t=6) I felt an inexpressible relief and a soothing conviction of protection and security when I recognized the stranger in the room, Mr. Lloyd, an apothecary.
(Day 2, t=7) Mr. Lloyd asked me, 'Well, who am I?' and I pronounced his name while offering him my hand.
(Day 2, t=8) Mr. Lloyd took my hand, smiled, and said, 'We shall do very well by-and-by.'
(Day 2, t=9) Mr. Lloyd laid me down and charged Bessie to be very careful that I was not disturbed during the night.
(Day 2, t=10) I felt grief when Mr. Lloyd departed, as I felt sheltered and befriended while he sat near my pillow.
(Day 2, t=11) Bessie asked me softly, 'Do you feel as if you should sleep, Miss?'
(Day 2, t=12) I scarcely dared to answer her, fearing the next sentence might be rough, and replied, 'I will try.'
(Day 2, t=13) Bessie asked if I would like to drink or could eat anything, and I responded, 'No, thank you, Bessie.'
(Day 2, t=14) Bessie said she would go to bed since it was past twelve o'clock, but I could call her if I wanted anything in the night.
(Day 2, t=15) I felt emboldened by Bessie's wonderful civility to ask, 'Bessie, what is the matter with me? Am I ill?'
(Day 2, t=16) Bessie explained that I fell sick in the red-room with crying and assured me I would be better soon.
(Day 2, t=17) I heard Bessie go into the housemaid's apartment and say, 'Sarah, come and sleep with me in the nursery; I daren’t for my life be alone with that poor child to-night: she might die.'
(Day 2, t=18) I caught scraps of Bessie's conversation with Sarah, inferring the main subject discussed was about strange occurrences.
(Day 2, t=19) I listened to Bessie and Sarah whispering together for half-an-hour before they fell asleep.
(Day 2, t=20) I experienced ghastly wakefulness throughout the long night, my ear, eye, and mind strained by dread.
(Day 2, t=21) I reflected that no severe or prolonged bodily illness followed the incident of the red-room, but it gave my nerves a shock.
(Day 2, t=22) I thought about Mrs. Reed and how I owed her some fearful pangs of mental suffering, but I ought to forgive her for she knew not what she did.
(Day 2, t=23) By noon the next day, I was up and dressed, sitting wrapped in a shawl by the nursery hearth.
(Day 2, t=24) I felt physically weak and broken down, but my worse ailment was an unutterable wretchedness of mind that drew silent tears from me.
(Day 2, t=25) I wiped one salt drop from my cheek, only for another to follow, despite thinking I ought to have been happy since none of the Reeds were there.
(Day 2, t=26) I noticed Abbot was sewing in another room, and Bessie addressed me with unwonted kindness as she moved about.
(Day 2, t=27) Bessie brought up a tart on a brightly painted china plate, which had often stirred in me a sense of admiration.
(Day 2, t=28) I was cordially invited to eat the circlet of delicate pastry upon the plate, but I could not eat the tart.
(Day 2, t=29) I put both the plate and the tart away, feeling that the plumage of the bird and the tints of the flowers seemed strangely faded.
(Day 2, t=30) Bessie asked if I would like a book, and the word 'book' acted as a transient stimulus, prompting me to ask her to fetch Gulliver’s Travels.
(Day 2, t=31) I considered Gulliver’s Travels a narrative of facts and found a deeper interest in it than in fairy tales.
(Day 2, t=32) I thought about how I had sought elves in vain and concluded they were gone to some savage country.
(Day 2, t=33) When Gulliver’s Travels was placed in my hand, I turned over its leaves, seeking the charm I had always found, but everything felt eerie and dreary.
(Day 2, t=34) I closed the book and put it on the table beside the untasted tart.
(Day 2, t=35) Bessie finished dusting and tidying the room, washed her hands, and began making a new bonnet for Georgiana’s doll.
(Day 2, t=36) I listened to Bessie sing a song about gipsying, which I had often heard before, but now found an indescribable sadness in its melody.
(Day 2, t=37) Bessie sang a doleful ballad about a poor orphan child, and I felt a deep connection to the lyrics.
(Day 2, t=38) Bessie finished her song and said, 'Come, Miss Jane, don’t cry,' but I felt as if she might as well have said to the fire, 'don’t burn!'
(Day 2, t=39) I felt morbid suffering that Bessie could not divine.
(Day 2, t=40) Mr. Lloyd came again in the course of the morning and remarked, 'What, already up!' as he entered the nursery.
(Day 2, t=41) Mr. Lloyd asked Bessie how I was doing, and she answered that I was doing very well.
(Day 2, t=42) Mr. Lloyd observed that I ought to look more cheerful and called me over, asking if my name was Jane.
(Day 2, t=43) I confirmed, 'Yes, sir, Jane Eyre.'
(Day 2, t=44) Mr. Lloyd noted that I had been crying and asked if I could tell him what about or if I had any pain.
(Day 2, t=45) I replied, 'No, sir,' feeling the weight of my emotions.
(Day 2, t=46) Bessie interjected, suggesting I was crying because I could not go out with Missis in the carriage.
(Day 2, t=47) I felt my self-esteem wounded by the false charge and promptly replied, 'I never cried for such a thing in my life: I hate going out in the carriage. I cry because I am miserable.'
(Day 2, t=48) Bessie exclaimed, 'Oh fie, Miss!' in response to my admission.
(Day 2, t=49) Mr. Lloyd appeared puzzled and fixed his eyes on me steadily, considering my situation.
(Day 2, t=50) I stood before Mr. Lloyd and explained, 'I was shut up in a room where there is a ghost till after dark.'
(Day 2, t=51) I saw Mr. Lloyd smile and frown at the same time, which made me feel uneasy.
(Day 2, t=52) I expressed my fear of Mr. Reed’s ghost, explaining that he died in that room and was laid out there.
(Day 2, t=53) I added that it was cruel to shut me up alone without a candle, 'so cruel that I think I shall never forget it.'
(Day 2, t=54) Mr. Lloyd dismissed my fears as nonsense and asked if that was what made me so miserable.
(Day 2, t=55) I admitted that I was unhappy for other things as well.
(Day 2, t=56) I wished to reply fully to Mr. Lloyd's question but found it difficult to frame an answer.
(Day 2, t=57) I managed to respond, 'For one thing, I have no father or mother, brothers or sisters.'
(Day 2, t=58) Mr. Lloyd reminded me that I had a kind aunt and cousins.
(Day 2, t=59) I paused and awkwardly said, 'But John Reed knocked me down, and my aunt shut me up in the red-room.'
(Day 2, t=60) Mr. Lloyd produced his snuff-box while listening to my explanation.
(Day 2, t=61) Mr. Lloyd asked if I thought Gateshead Hall was a very beautiful house and if I was thankful to have such a fine place to live at.
(Day 2, t=62) I replied, 'It is not my house, sir; and Abbot says I have less right to be here than a servant.'
(Day 2, t=63) Mr. Lloyd questioned if I was silly enough to wish to leave such a splendid place.
(Day 2, t=64) I expressed that if I had anywhere else to go, I would be glad to leave it, but I could never get away from Gateshead until I was a woman.
(Day 2, t=65) Mr. Lloyd asked if I had any relations besides Mrs. Reed.
(Day 2, t=66) I reflected that I did not know and mentioned that Aunt Reed once said I might have some poor, low relations called Eyre.
(Day 2, t=67) Mr. Lloyd inquired if I would like to go to them, and I reflected on the grimness of poverty.
(Day 2, t=68) I replied, 'No; I should not like to belong to poor people.'
(Day 2, t=69) Mr. Lloyd asked if I would like to go to school.
(Day 2, t=70) I reflected that I scarcely knew what school was, but Bessie’s accounts of it were somewhat appalling yet attractive.
(Day 2, t=71) I concluded, 'I should indeed like to go to school.'
(Day 2, t=72) Mr. Lloyd remarked that I ought to have change of air and scene, speaking to himself as he got up.
(Day 2, t=73) Bessie returned just as the carriage was heard rolling up the gravel-walk.
(Day 2, t=74) Mr. Lloyd asked Bessie if that was her mistress and expressed a desire to speak to her before he left.
(Day 2, t=75) Bessie invited Mr. Lloyd to walk into the breakfast-room and led the way out.
(Day 3, t=0) I gathered enough hope from my discourse with Mr. Lloyd and the conference between Bessie and Abbot to wish to get well.
(Day 3, t=1) I desired and waited in silence for a change that tarried, while days and weeks passed.
(Day 3, t=2) I regained my normal state of health but no new allusion was made to the subject I brooded over.
(Day 3, t=3) I observed Mrs. Reed surveying me with a severe eye but seldom addressing me.
(Day 3, t=4) I was appointed a small closet to sleep in by myself and condemned to take my meals alone.
(Day 3, t=5) I passed all my time in the nursery while my cousins were in the drawing-room.
(Day 3, t=6) I felt an instinctive certainty that Mrs. Reed would not long endure me under the same roof.
(Day 3, t=7) I noticed that Mrs. Reed's glance expressed an insuperable and rooted aversion when turned on me.
(Day 3, t=8) I observed Eliza and Georgiana speaking to me as little as possible, evidently acting according to orders.
(Day 3, t=9) I saw John thrust his tongue in his cheek whenever he saw me and attempt chastisement.
(Day 3, t=10) I instantly turned against John, roused by deep ire and desperate revolt.
(Day 3, t=11) I struck John hard on the nose, and when I saw that my look daunted him, I had the inclination to follow up my advantage.
(Day 3, t=12) I heard John run from me, uttering execrations and vowing I had burst his nose.
(Day 3, t=13) I heard John in a blubbering tone commence the tale of how 'that nasty Jane Eyre' had flown at him like a mad cat.
(Day 3, t=14) I suddenly cried out, leaning over the banister, 'They are not fit to associate with me.'
(Day 3, t=15) Mrs. Reed ran nimbly up the stair, swept me into the nursery, and crushed me down on the edge of my crib.
(Day 3, t=16) Mrs. Reed dared me in an emphatic voice to rise from that place or utter one syllable during the remainder of the day.
(Day 3, t=17) I demanded, 'What would Uncle Reed say to you, if he were alive?'
(Day 3, t=18) I felt as if my tongue pronounced words without my will consenting to their utterance.
(Day 3, t=19) I told Mrs. Reed that my Uncle Reed is in heaven and can see all she does and thinks.
(Day 3, t=20) I stated that Uncle Reed knows how she shuts me up all day long and wishes me dead.
(Day 3, t=21) Mrs. Reed shook me soundly, boxed both my ears, and then left me without a word.
(Day 3, t=22) Bessie supplied the hiatus with a homily of an hour’s length, proving I was the most wicked and abandoned child ever reared under a roof.
(Day 3, t=23) I half believed Bessie, feeling only bad feelings surging in my breast.
(Day 3, t=24) I witnessed the daily apparelling of Eliza and Georgiana, who descended to the drawing-room dressed in thin muslin frocks and scarlet sashes.
(Day 3, t=25) I listened to the sound of the piano or harp played below, the passing to and fro of the butler and footman, and the jingling of glass and china.
(Day 3, t=26) I retired from the stairhead to the solitary and silent nursery, feeling somewhat sad but not miserable.
(Day 3, t=27) I had no wish to go into company, as I was rarely noticed there.
(Day 3, t=28) I wished Bessie had been kind and companionable, as I would have deemed it a treat to spend the evenings quietly with her.
(Day 3, t=29) I sat with my doll on my knee till the fire got low, glancing round occasionally to ensure nothing worse than myself haunted the shadowy room.
(Day 3, t=30) I undressed hastily, tugging at knots and strings, and sought shelter from cold and darkness in my crib.
(Day 3, t=31) I took my doll to my crib, believing it to be happy and feeling comparatively happy myself.
(Day 3, t=32) I waited long for the departure of the company and listened for the sound of Bessie’s step on the stairs.
(Day 3, t=33) I would sometimes see Bessie come up to seek her thimble or scissors or bring me something by way of supper.
(Day 3, t=34) I ate the supper Bessie brought me while she sat on the bed.
(Day 3, t=35) Bessie tucked the clothes around me, kissed me twice, and said, 'Good night, Miss Jane.'
(Day 3, t=36) I wished intensely that Bessie would always be pleasant and amiable and never scold or task me unreasonably.
(Day 3, t=37) I remembered Bessie as a slim young woman with black hair, dark eyes, and nice features.
(Day 3, t=38) I thought Bessie had a capricious and hasty temper and indifferent ideas of principle or justice.
(Day 3, t=39) I preferred Bessie to anyone else at Gateshead Hall.
(Day 3, t=40) On the fifteenth of January, about nine o’clock in the morning, I was making my bed.
(Day 3, t=41) I had received strict orders from Bessie to get my bed arranged before she returned.
(Day 3, t=42) I spread the quilt and folded my night-dress before going to the window-seat.
(Day 3, t=43) I put in order some picture-books and doll’s house furniture scattered there.
(Day 3, t=44) I was stopped by an abrupt command from Georgiana to let her playthings alone.
(Day 3, t=45) I fell to breathing on the frost-flowers on the window, clearing a space to look out on the grounds.
(Day 3, t=46) I saw the gates thrown open and a carriage roll through, which I watched ascending the drive with indifference.
(Day 3, t=47) I noticed the carriage stopped in front of the house and the door-bell rang loudly.
(Day 3, t=48) I saw the new-comer admitted but found it nothing to me.
(Day 3, t=49) I found livelier attraction in the spectacle of a little hungry robin chirping on the twigs of the leafless cherry-tree.
(Day 3, t=50) I crumbled a morsel of roll from my breakfast and tugged at the sash to put out the crumbs on the window-sill.
(Day 3, t=51) I scattered the crumbs on the stone sill and the cherry-tree bough before closing the window.
(Day 3, t=52) I replied to Bessie, 'No, I have only just finished dusting,' after she asked if I had washed my hands and face.
(Day 3, t=53) I gave another tug at the sash before answering Bessie.
(Day 3, t=54) Bessie hauled me to the washstand and inflicted a merciless scrub on my face and hands.
(Day 3, t=55) Bessie disciplined my head with a bristly brush and denuded me of my pinafore.
(Day 3, t=56) Bessie hurried me to the top of the stairs and bid me go down directly as I was wanted in the breakfast-room.
(Day 3, t=57) I slowly descended the stairs, intimidated and trembling, as I had not been called to Mrs. Reed’s presence for nearly three months.
(Day 3, t=58) I stood in the empty hall, intimidated by the breakfast-room door.
(Day 3, t=59) I stood in agitated hesitation for ten minutes before the vehement ringing of the breakfast-room bell decided me to enter.
(Day 3, t=60) I asked inwardly, 'Who could want me?' as I turned the stiff door-handle.
(Day 3, t=61) I passed through the door, curtsied low, and looked up at a black pillar, which was a man standing on the rug.
(Day 3, t=62) Mrs. Reed introduced me to the stony stranger, saying, 'This is the little girl respecting whom I applied to you.'
(Day 3, t=63) I stood before Mr. Brocklehurst as he examined me with inquisitive-looking grey eyes.
(Day 3, t=64) I told Mr. Brocklehurst my name was 'Jane Eyre.'
